thread.title: Why Louisiana might send more youth to adult prisons | State Politics | theadvocate.com
thread.title_full: Why Louisiana might send more youth to adult prisons | State Politics | theadvocate.com

author: Meghan Friedmann
title: Why Louisiana might send more youth to adult prisons | State Politics | theadvocate.com
text:
The Louisiana Legislature on Friday passed [a bill that could ultimately land more juveniles in adult prisons](https://www.nola.com/news/politics/louisiana-senate-advances-bill-that-could-land-more-teens-in-adult-prison/article_8077ab3c-a2d7-11ef-9f54-f3a87cfacc3a.html), a measure championed by prosecutors but fiercely criticized by criminal justice advocates.
Senate Bill 2, by state Sen. Heather Cloud, R-Turkey Creek, and state Rep. Debbie Villio, R-Kenner, passed the House in a 70-25 vote, with opposition coming from Democrats. The bill had already passed the Senate.
SB 2 seeks to change the Constitution by giving the Legislature power to increase the number of crimes for which juveniles may be transferred to adult court. Though the original version of SB 2 would have allowed the Legislature to make any crime transferable, but a House amendment limited that power to only apply to felony offenses.
Currently, the Constitution lists 16 transferable crimes. If the Legislature wants to add to that list, they must do so via a constitutional amendment, meaning state voters must approve it.
Murder, attempted murder, manslaughter, aggravated kidnapping, rape, armed robbery and a second offense of burglary of an inhabited dwelling are among the crimes on that list.
SB 2 — which the public also must approve, as it is itself a constitutional amendment — would remove that list of crimes from the Constitution and allow legislators to add crimes by a two-thirds vote, without public approval.
Proponents argue the change would give the Legislature more flexibility to change the law and give prosecutors more tools to fight juvenile crime. Critics argue the Constitution already covers the most serious crimes and protects children from being sent to adult prison where it is harder for them to rehabilitate.
Those two stances were on show during a Nov. 18 meeting of the Committee on the Administration of Criminal Justice.
Villio, who chairs that committee, complained that the current list doesn’t include crimes such as carjacking. If a prosecutor wants to send a youth to adult court, she said, the charge must be armed robbery.
She also complained that home invasion is not a transferable crime, though armed robbery and a second offense of burglary of an inhabited dwelling are.
Hillar Moore, the district attorney for East Baton Rouge Parish, and Tony Clayton, the district attorney for the 18th Judicial District, both supported the bill.
“There’s a new juvenile out there ... they will not leave witnesses, folks,” Clayton said.
He added that he would not use the transfer authority to harm innocent children.
“As a district attorney, give me the power to make the decision,” he said.
Criminal justice reform advocates argued that sending youths to adult prison, where they don’t have the same access to the rehabilitative programs as the juvenile justice system offers, often leaves them worse off and can make them vulnerable to rape by adults.
A federal mandate requires children 17 and under in adult facilities to be sight and sound separated from adults. But not every jail has the space or set-up to easily comply with that rule.
Hannah Van De Car, deputy director of the Louisiana Center for Children’s rights, said she has represented kids in adult jails across the state.
“Sight and sound separation is supposed to happen under federal mandate, but that doesn’t always occur. If it does, it’s in solitary confinement. I’ve also had other clients who’ve been shoved together in a single cell,” Van De Car told the committee. “I had a client who had to sleep on the floor of a jail cell while cockroaches crawled on top of him, because there were no beds left for him.”
Darryl Hurst, an East Baton Rouge Parish councilman who spoke against SB 2, said that until the state addresses the root causes of crime, like education and poverty, “y’all are wasting y’all’s time.”
“The only reason you’re not worried about it is because your kids will never go to jail just like mine won’t,” he told the panel, contending that folks who can afford good representation are far more likely to keep their children out of jail.
Hurst also alluded to the likelihood that the bill would disproportionately affect the Black community.
“It does affect kids in my community that I represent, which is 92% African American,” he said.
Louisiana’s youth justice system also disproportionately impacts Black youth. As of 2021, Black youth were 4.7 times more likely to be admitted to youth facilities as White youth, according to a report from The Sentencing Project, a nonprofit that urges alternatives to imprisonment.
